competitors.label: Props
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue explanation of one-way data flow, readonly props, and when local copies or transforms are appropriate.
theirStrengths: The official props guide is authoritative on one-way data flow, readonly behavior, and the two accepted local-copy or transform patterns.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about nested object mutation bugs and the interview/debug framing around ambiguous ownership.

realWorldUseCases: This couples child behavior to parent state and makes bugs look “random” because the mutation didn’t go through an explicit parent update.
actionableExamples: The child requests a change; the parent applies it.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain that props refresh from the parent and should not be mutated, but they are less vivid about hard-to-trace shared-reference bugs.
actionableExamples: They show the official local-copy and derived-transform patterns, so actionable coverage is still strong.
followUpConfusion: The official guide remains stronger on the exact supported alternatives when you need local state derived from a prop.
Add one tighter note on when a local ref copy is appropriate versus when emit or v-model is the better contract.

competitors.winReason: The official docs are canonical on one-way flow, but our page is stronger on the nested-object trap and on turning the rule into a practical debugging explanation.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because prop flow, state ownership, and component communication are core interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong broad Vue interview-prep hub with component-communication coverage.
theirGaps: The landing page is not a direct prop-mutation explainer, so the nested-object pitfall is less explicit.

realWorldUseCases: Objects/arrays are passed by reference; child mutation becomes parent mutation
followUpConfusion: Correct approach: child emits intent (update events / v-model) and the parent owns the actual state mutation.

realWorldUseCases: The bank covers Vue component patterns broadly, but it is less focused on this specific shared-reference bug.
actionableExamples: Because GreatFrontEnd emphasizes worked answers, the example bar stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about readonly props, object mutation leaks, and v-model as the clean update contract.

actionableExamples: Keep the local-draft example short so the page remains focused on ownership rather than form architecture.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is a strong broad Vue resource, but our page is more focused on why prop mutation creates ambiguous ownership and how to replace it with emit or v-model.

competitors.label: Vue Error: Avoid Mutating a Prop Directly
competitors.selectionReason: Michael Thiessen has a topic-direct article on the exact prop-mutation warning and its correct alternatives.
theirStrengths: It is a direct focused explanation of the prop-mutation warning and how to replace the anti-pattern.
theirGaps: It is lighter on the nested object or array shared-reference trap and on the parent-owned-state mental model.

realWorldUseCases: Mutates a nested field on an object/array prop (e.g. props.user.name = 'x') You silently mutate the parent’s object (shared reference) and create “action at a distance” bugs
followUpConfusion: Mutating props breaks one-way data flow: the parent is no longer the single source of truth, updates become non-deterministic to trace, and nested object/array props can mutate parent state by reference.

realWorldUseCases: The article addresses the warning directly, but it is less broad on shared-reference bugs beyond the main anti-pattern.
actionableExamples: It includes a clear replacement pattern, so examples remain close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about nested objects, local draft state, and the parent-child ownership contract.

actionableExamples: Keep one very short nested-object example so the shared-reference bug stays visible without making the answer too long.

competitors.winReason: Michael Thiessen is a strong topic-direct comparator, but our page is stronger on the nested-object trap and the one-source-of-truth explanation that interviewers often probe next.
